method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is a small, uninhabited coral island in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, located about 40 km south of the Equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W.[2] It is part of the central Line Islands and consists of the exposed crest of an ancient coral reef cap over an extinct volcanic base.[2]

The island has an emergent land area of about 4.5 km², with low relief and a highest point of approximately 7 m above sea level.[2] Its center is a dried former lagoon in which deep guano deposits accumulated, a feature that strongly influenced the island’s later environmental history.[2] Jarvis has a tropical desert climate characterized by high daytime temperatures, persistent easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sun, and low annual rainfall of less than 1 m.[2] No natural freshwater lens is known from the island.[2]

The terrestrial surface is mostly sandy and supports only sparse vegetation, including bunch grasses, vines, and low-growing shrubs, with no plants larger than shrubs.[2] These limited plant communities provide nesting, roosting, and shelter habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and numerous land hermit crabs.[2] Jarvis has been described by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as one of the last places where tropical island terrestrial and marine ecosystems remain relatively intact and comparatively free of human impact.[3]

Jarvis Island is especially important for seabirds and has more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] Background material from U.S. agencies has estimated that Jarvis alone supports around 1 million sooty terns.[1] The refuge planning documents also describe Jarvis as having 14 species of breeding seabirds and as the only protected seabird island in the northern Line Islands.[3]

The surrounding marine ecosystem is noted for exceptional productivity caused by the combined effects of wind-driven equatorial upwelling and topographic upwelling around the island.[2] Shallow benthic habitats include a fringing reef, shallow back-reef areas, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side.[2] Refuge documents describe the fringing reef as a healthy coral community whose condition is related to the island’s remoteness and limited anthropogenic disturbance.[3]

Jarvis’s ecology was heavily altered during the era of guano extraction, which removed nutrient deposits and contributed to major environmental impacts.[1] Introduced cats and rats further disrupted the island’s seabird community.[1] A later assessment of recovery on Baker, Howland, and Jarvis reported that, following eradication efforts, seabird numbers increased on all three islands, though the magnitude varied by island.[1] For Jarvis specifically, the recovery reportedly included the return of both shearwater species after the removal of invasive predators.[1]

==History==
Jarvis Island was first sighted by Europeans on 21 August 1821, when Captain Brown, sailing on the British ship *Eliza Francis*, recorded the island.[1][2][4][5] The island was named for the owners of the vessel, Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis.[2]

Interest in Jarvis increased after American mariners identified valuable guano deposits, leading to a U.S. survey of the island in the early 1840s.[1][2] In 1857, Alfred G. Benson and Charles H. Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner *Liholiho*, took possession of Jarvis for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2][4] Later that year, Commander Charles Henry Davis of USS *St. Mary* surveyed the island and formally claimed it for the United States.[1][4]

Guano extraction began in the late 1850s and continued through the late nineteenth century.[1][4][5] By the time the island was abandoned in 1879, about 8,000 tonnes of guano had been removed.[1] The United Kingdom annexed Jarvis in 1889, but did not substantially develop the island or resume mining on a significant scale.[1][4][5] In 1906, the island was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London, although no major new mining operations followed.[4]

The United States reoccupied and reclaimed Jarvis in March 1935.[1][4][5] President Franklin D. Roosevelt then issued Executive Order 7368 on 13 May 1936, placing Jarvis under the jurisdiction of the U.S. Secretary of the Interior.[3] The island was originally administered by the Office of Territorial Affairs.[3]

During the Second World War, Japanese forces attacked Jarvis in 1942, prompting the evacuation of its colonists.[1] On 27 June 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated Jarvis Island and its territorial sea out to 3 nautical miles as part of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was also included within the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==Transportation==
Jarvis Island has no permanent transportation infrastructure, and access is limited to marine vessels capable of extended open-ocean travel.[3] The island is visited only by ship, with recent transportation provided by NOAA vessels, the United States Coast Guard, and private charter vessels.[3] Jarvis lies about 1,263 nautical miles from Honolulu, and travel by ship typically takes about eight days.[3] Refuge planning documents identify the lack of affordable and reliable transportation, limited field infrastructure, and the difficulty of landing safely as major operational constraints.[3]  

The island’s physical geography further complicates access and landing operations.[1] Jarvis is a low-lying, nearly level island surrounded by a narrow, shallow fringing reef, beyond which submarine slopes descend steeply into deep water.[1] The western, leeward shore is sandy and low, while the eastern side is more abrupt and exposed to persistent wave action generated by the trade winds.[1]  

Because transportation is entirely sea-based, management and research activities depend on periodic ship expeditions.[3][5] NOAA scientific cruises have regularly visited Jarvis for monitoring work, including voyages by the NOAA ships Townsend Cromwell and Oscar Elton Sette.[5] Refuge planning has also considered the use of a U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service-owned vessel to support rotating field camps among remote island refuges, though public access would remain closed.[3]

==Military==
Jarvis Island had strategic significance in the interwar period because the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project sought to place U.S. citizens on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker so that weather stations and landing fields could be developed for military and commercial aviation routes between Australia and California.[1] The project was also intended in part to strengthen United States territorial claims in the central Pacific as a check on Japanese expansion in the region.[1]

Beginning in 1935, Jarvis served as one of the islands occupied by Hui Panalāʻau, a group composed primarily of young Native Hawaiian men and other male students recruited in Hawaii.[1] A later U.S. Army-sponsored presentation on the documentary Under a Jarvis Moon described these settlements on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker as part of U.S. efforts from 1935 to 1942 to colonize uninhabited Pacific islands, and stated that more than 130 young men from Hawaii lived on the three islands, including 52 Hawaiians.[2]

Physical traces of this period remained visible in the island’s surrounding reef environment, where short channels were blasted through the fringing reef to facilitate small-boat access for historical mining and military activities.[4] Later refuge planning documents also referred to debris and hazards associated with human habitation during the guano-mining and military eras.[3]

The colonization effort ended in early 1942 after Japanese attacks prompted the evacuation of the island’s settlers.[1] In the postwar period, Jarvis had no permanent military installation, and access came to depend instead on occasional transport by agencies such as NOAA and the U.S. Coast Guard during scientific and management visits.[3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974 under the administration of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service.[2][5] Early refuge planning stated that management should preserve the island’s “complete ecosystem,” with particular emphasis on its large seabird nesting colonies.[5]

The refuge was originally defined to include the island and its territorial sea out to 3 nautical miles, comprising 1,273 acres of emergent land and 36,214 acres of submerged lands and waters, for a total of 37,487 acres.[5] U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service materials now describe the refuge as extending over submerged lands out to 200 nautical miles from the island and encompassing 429,853 acres, of which 428,580 acres are submerged and 1,273 acres are terrestrial.[2] In the World Database on Protected Areas, Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge is classified as a marine and coastal protected area with a reported area of 1,744.87 km².[1]

Management of the refuge is guided by the mission of the National Wildlife Refuge System, which is to conserve, manage, and where appropriate restore fish, wildlife, plant resources, and their habitats for present and future generations.[2][5] A comprehensive conservation plan for the refuge was prepared by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in 2008 as part of broader planning for several central Pacific refuges.[4]

Since 2009, the refuge has also lain within the boundaries of the Pacific Islands Heritage Marine National Monument, referred to in earlier documents as the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[2][3] The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has continued periodic biological and management visits, including a 2024 expedition described as the agency’s first trip to Jarvis in more than six years.[3]

Recent refuge administration has included review of limited access proposals judged against compatibility with conservation purposes; for example, in 2023 the Fish and Wildlife Service issued a draft compatibility determination on amateur radio operations and concluded that such access would not detract from the refuge’s core conservation mission if managed under specified guidelines.[2]
